The grip of the grave took hold of me. I came to grief and sorrow.<|10.96|> <|12.98|> Disorientation, despair, grief, lament, sorrow. and thus is not startled or scandalized<|26.28|> <|26.28|> by our expressions of disorientation<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.10|> grief, or pain.<|2.60|> <|4.18|> Repressing that God's, it is in our honest admissions of disorientation<|23.94|> <|23.94|> that God's mercy can be most intensely experienced.<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.56|> It almost theories,<|7.02|> <|7.02|> false solutions, and armchair epidemiological musings.<|11.24|> <|12.62|> These times are disorienting.<|15.74|> <|16.72|> And it is not only okay to admit this before God,<|21.02|> <|21.34|> but we must join the psalmist in saying it aloud.<|26.10|> <|27.28|> These are disorienting times.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.54|> And when we say that aloud, when we admit that with our lips, we are joining good company.<|8.98|> <|10.54|> And numbered in that company are the two disciples making the seven-mile trek between Jerusalem to Emmaus.<|19.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What is often lost about this story is that it occurs on the same day of Jesus' quiet,<|9.34|> <|9.34|> almost unnoticed Yumoas takes place at a rest stop food court,<|16.00|> <|16.00|> and Christ's blessing is pronounced over KFC combos.<|21.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In that painting, Mary and Cleopas' disorientation is palpable.<|7.88|> <|9.10|> Holding paper cups embossed with the head of Colonel Sanders, Mary and Cleopas are shell-shocked.<|16.30|> some people we know in Jerusalem<|14.00|> <|14.00|> have said that this same Jesus whose trial and crucifixion we witnessed,<|19.00|> <|19.00|> yes, him, people are saying that he's alive.<|24.00|> tragically and prematurely and they've said their last goodbyes<|12.82|> <|12.82|> and are trying to pick up the pieces of their shattered dreams and expectations.<|16.56|> <|17.26|> Isn't that usually how grief works?<|19.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A loved one dies unexpectedly or not.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> And we are left trying to make sense of not only being able to pick up the phone,<|9.00|> <|9.00|> of not being able to pick up the phone and hear their voice or visit them over the holidays<|14.00|> <|14.00|> and hear the same stories they always
